paragraphs.text: News and Notes contains expanded treatment of archaeological issues, descriptions of unusual artifacts and sites, summaries of fieldwork, SCA organizational and awards news, important new legislation, and an honoring of our colleagues who have passed on. This edition includes information on the discovery of
the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island
was reported to have lived during her 18 years of isolation.
was found working in a brush covered windbreak on the west end of
, but early accounts state she was actually living in a cave nearby.
was explored in the years immediately after her discovery, but the location of

The key piece of information that led to the discovery of
was an 1879 U.S. Coast Survey map that shows the location of "
." However, the map was not detailed enough to pinpoint the cave's location precisely as the shoreline is actually more complex than shown on the map and

had become completely buried, with no visible trace of its location. Testing in the area for more than 20 years failed to uncover any evidence of a cave or any occupation for that matter, but one location always looked promising. The break in the story came when
, discovered the field notes that accompany the 1879 U.S. Coast Survey map. The field notes specify that
on the map was, in fact,

was believed to have lived, and the map includes a detailed drawing showing precise distance and compass bearing from the closest survey station (the location of which could be approximated). This pointed to the same spot that always seemed the most likely location for

must be at this spot, but was likely deeply buried, far deeper than anyone had expected. A field crew consisting of
archaeology students made a herculean effort to remove an estimated one million pounds of sand, all by hand, one bucket load at a time.
had become completely buried by windborne and waterborne sediments. The early history of
saw the development of a sheep ranch in 1857, which triggered an era of extreme erosion due to loss of vegetation. This placed large amounts of blowing sand into the system, which allowed
to be completely buried within a short period of time. The top of
entrance was buried under two meters of sand, with a total of seven meters deposited over the presumed 1850s floor of
. The first evidence of occupation encountered were two sets of initials with the date of September 11, 1911, indicating that

was at least still partially open at that time. A midden layer with dark soil, shell, bone, and lithics was encountered at seven meters below the surface. Excavations were stopped at that point, pending review and approval by

is an ancient sea cave in an 80,000-year-old marine terrace, which has been uplifted a few meters above sea level.

during historic times, as well as serving as a cap providing incredible preservation of the prehistoric deposits beneath. Future work in
